data.title: Oakmark Global Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth-quarter highlights

Top contributors

Samsung Electronics (SSNLF)

DSV (DSDVF)

IQVIA Holdings (IQV)

Top detractors

Alibaba Group (BABA)

CNH Industrial (CNH)

Charter Communications Cl A (CHTR)

New purchases

Gartner (IT)

Mondelēz Cl A (MDLZ)

Final sales

General Motors (GM)

Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF)

  • The Fund (Investor Class)(OAKGX) outperformed the benchmark, the MSCI World Index, for the quarter, the calendar year and since inception.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were health care and information technology, while consumer staples was the only detractor.
  • Geographically, the top three regional allocations are 51.2% in the U.S., 39.0% in Europe ex U.K. and 5.6% in the U.K. For the quarter, the U.S., Switzerland and South Korea were the largest contributors to performance. China, the Netherlands and Sweden were the largest detractors from performance. Emerging markets accounted for 4.3% of the portfolio.

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Samsung Electronics was the top contributor during the quarter. The South Korea-headquartered tech company’s stock price rose as earnings staged a sharp recovery due to strength in its core semiconductor business. We recently met with the company in South Korea and were reassured by its confidence in its High Bandwidth Memory product lineup, which has continued to improve under new management. We continue to believe Samsung is one of the world’s leading semiconductor companies with a long runway for future growth.

Top detractor

Alibaba Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The China-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price declined as it reported mixed results. The core E-commerce business continues to perform well, and Cloud revenue growth is accelerating. However, the company’s earnings were negatively impacted due to significant spending on subsidies to grow their Quick Commerce business, in our opinion. We believe losses from Quick Commerce will be reduced over time and continue to believe the company is well-positioned for long-term growth, having been one of the early investors in Chinese AI. Over time, we think it can leverage its advanced capabilities and leading market position to unlock further value.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

Gartner is a global leader in research services, with a long history of delivering valuable insights and data to business and technology leaders. In our view, the company has the best brand in IT research, supported by its scale and a compelling customer value proposition. These advantages have driven a long history of strong organic growth and robust free-cash-flow conversion. The stock price has declined meaningfully from recent highs due to investor concerns surrounding AI-related disruption. We believe these concerns are overstated. In our view, Gartner is well-positioned to reaccelerate organic growth due to continued high customer engagement and the large opportunity to sell to new and existing customers. We took advantage of the opportunity to buy shares in this well-managed company at a bargain price.

Mondelēz International is a global snacking powerhouse with leading market share positions in crackers, cookies and chocolate. The brand portfolio houses iconic names like Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, Oreo and Ritz. Mondelēz possesses a unique global footprint that over-indexes to snacking occasions. Snacking is an advantaged category that benefits from robust pricing power, low private label competition and rising per capita consumption. We believe these attributes will help Mondelēz sustain industry-leading growth. A rapid rise in commodity costs has temporarily depressed margins, masking the company’s true earnings power. We believe Mondelēz’s strong pricing power and commodity relief will help improve margins. The short-term fears surrounding commodity inflation allowed us to purchase shares at a discounted valuation relative to history, peers and the broader market.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: Strong Cash Flow And Growing Profitability
data.description:
Since my last bullish article on Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) in March 2024, the stock has been on a rollercoaster ride. It reached a five-year low in January 2025 due to weak demand concerns before rebounding over the last 12 months, increasing by 42.75%. Today's market cap makes it the world's largest brewer and fourth-largest beverage company, indicating that its efforts to overcome a flat beer market are beginning to show results.

It has beaten EPS expectations for the last sixteen consecutive quarters, is nearing $11 billion in positive levered free cash flow in FY2025, and is slowly reducing its debt intake. At the same time, it is investing to improve cost control across the supply chain. Revenue growth is still flat, but premium pricing and brand strength continue to support profitability. The company is scheduled to release its Q4 2025 results on February 12, 2026. On top of that, the company still appears undervalued relative to beverage peers. Therefore, I maintain a cautiously bullish stance.

Company Updates

AB InBev is the world's largest brewer and owns global and local megabrands, 21 of which generate over $1 billion in annual revenue. Like many peers, AB InBev has been experiencing flat and declining volume of beer consumed in its mature markets, while emerging markets still show growth. According to Mordor Intelligence, the global beer market is expected to grow by 5.16% through 2031 to reach $1.09 trillion. Most of this growth is expected to be attained through higher pricing strategies and non-alcohol variants.

AB InBev is focused on building megabrands, premiumising its portfolio, and expanding its scope of beverage variants outside traditional beer across mature and developing markets.

Past acquisitions expanded the company's scale but also resulted in a highly leveraged balance sheet. Recent results show improving profitability and cash flow alongside debt reduction. One of the most recent actions was to take more control of its input costs by reacquiring a metal container plant in a $3 billion deal. The company also invests in long-term partnerships, such as the upcoming FIFA World Cup tournament, to help maintain its premium positioning and pricing power, rather than directly driving revenue growth. For FY 2025 guidance, management has indicated organic EBITDA growth of between 4% and 8%.

Financials

Anheuser-Busch InBev revenue growth has been flat to low single-digit growth, with trailing twelve months (TTM) at $58.605 billion. To some extent, this is what you can expect from a mature consumer staples company. Premiumisation and cost discipline have helped gross profit margins improve annually since FY 2023.

If we look at net income over the years, we can see that it has improved annually since FY2023. However, historically, it has been much higher. A big part of the net income is reduced due to the company's high-interest expenses from the debt it has taken on to make those previously massive acquisitions.

Cash flow is one of the big reasons investors are attracted to this company. It has had trending positive levered free cash flow upward since FY2023, and TTM is at $10.164 billion. With this, the company can reinvest, pay off debts, and reward investors. It pays a semiannual dividend program with a TTM yield of 1.97%. However, the yield is small relative to other beverage peers.

The company has taken on a lot of debt but has been reducing it year after year. We can see that total debt is currently at $75.84 billion. This high debt does limit the company's flexibility and requires it to continue performing.

Valuation

Comparing AB InBev to the largest beverage peers on the stock market, namely The Coca-Cola Company (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), Starbucks (SBUX), Monster Beverage (MNST), Diageo (DEO), and Heineken (HEINY). We can see that it is considered the largest brewer by enterprise value of $207.068 billion.

Although the stock has had some serious momentum over the last year. If we look at the relative peer valuation, the company is still considered cheap at a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) of 19.07. AB InBev is impacted by beer headwinds, high debt, and lower growth compared to some of the higher-valued largely non-alcoholic beverage peers. Only Diageo and Heineken are lower, with 13.93 and 16.73.

If we take it one step further and include the growth, we can see that AB InBev has a PEG of 1.72, which is lower than its peers, suggesting the stock is not priced for aggressive growth. On the positive side, AB InBev has strong brands, huge cash flow, and its balance sheet is improving. This indicates that there could still be upside potential. We can see that Ab InBev has had a flat 3-year revenue CAGR of 0.74%, but the company continues to invest in operational improvement opportunities and has reiterated confidence in FY2025 financials.

Risks

One of the key challenges for AB InBev is that its largest product, beer, is in structural decline, with no real indication of any long-term turnaround. The beer market growth is expected at a CAGR of 5.16%, according to research; however, this is due to premium pricing and non-alcohol variations. Another issue is the large debt load, which to some extent limits the company's potential for capital returns. Any mistakes the company makes throughout its operations hurt more when it is this leveraged. This is obviously a little bit risky in an industry in which consumer habits have drastically changed, and AB InBev, to some extent, is still finding its footing. Furthermore, as a global company, there are geopolitical factors such as taxes, regulations, and political instability that could negatively impact the company's performance.

Final Thoughts

AB InBev is positioning itself as not only the globe's largest beverage company but also as one that is providing beyond-beer and non-alcohol products to customers across the globe. It is doing this through major acquisitions, which have increased market share but also make the company debt-heavy. This is a sturdy business that is interesting for its successful cash generation, growing profitability, and financial discipline. The company has proven that it can handle a changing consumer demand environment and still appears undervalued relative to beverage peers. Therefore, I maintain a cautiously bullish long-term view on the stock.

data.title: Mont Belvieu Surges In Significance Amid Record U.S. NGL Exports
data.description:
By Paul Wightman, Elizabeth Hui and Cameron Liao

Like Cushing in Oklahoma rising to global prominence as the benchmark for spot crude oil prices, Mont Belvieu on the Texas Gulf Coast has cemented its standing as the primary pricing point for natural gas liquids (NGLs) used in the petrochemical and energy sectors worldwide.

Strategically located 30 miles east of Houston, Mont Belvieu is the cornerstone for NGL processing and trade. The area's unique naturally occurring salt dome formations provide essential underground storage with a capacity of over 400 million barrels, creating a massive, low-cost storage facility for NGLs such as propane (used for home heating and backyard grilling), ethane (a primary feedstock for ethylene that is converted to polyethylene used in plastic bottles), butane and iso-butane (used as feedstocks and blend stocks in transportation fuels).

Pipelines strategically connect fractionation units – responsible for separating NGL mixtures extracted from natural gas wells into individual components such as ethane, propane and butane – to major NGL consumers across the U.S. as well as to international markets via the import/export terminal on the Houston Ship Channel. Owners and operators of fractionators and storage facilities include Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products (EPD), ONEOK (OKE) and Targa Resources (TRGP). These firms collectively operate a total of 36 fractionation units with a capacity of over 4 million barrels per day.

Since the shale revolution began in 2008, the Mont Belvieu infrastructure has undergone significant expansion. This was driven by a surge in NGL production from the Permian, Eagle Ford and Bakken regions, which led to increased demand from petrochemical industries and a rise in exports to destinations in Europe and Asia. According to the latest data from Vortexa, the biggest international buyers of NGLs were China, Japan, Indonesia and South Korea, alongside the Netherlands, Sweden, the United Kingdom, Spain and Norway. NGLs continue to be a bright spot for growth, contrasting with crude oil, which appears to remain stagnant in 2026.

Total U.S. propane exports look set to exceed 1.9 or 2-million barrels per day, an increase of about 10% year-on-year compared to 2024 levels. The latest data from the ship tracking firm Vortexa shows that propane exports have been rising year-on-year for the past several years. Across all the NGLs, the latest forecast from the U.S. EIA shows that total production is expected to reach 7.32 million barrels per day in 2025 and 7.46 million barrels per day for 2026, an increase of around 2% year-on-year.

The increasing adoption of Mont Belvieu pricing as a global benchmark is underscored by the commitment of Indian national oil companies (NOCs) to utilize OPIS Mont Belvieu prices for U.S. origin liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) shipments of propane and butane scheduled for delivery in 2026. This aligns with India’s aim of sourcing 10% of its LPG from the United States.

Strong Financial Links to Mont Belvieu

The growth in U.S. NGL exports to Europe and Asia has helped deepen financial ties back to Mont Belvieu, similar to how U.S. exports of crude oil strengthened the relationship to NYMEX WTI and natural gas to Henry Hub.

The growth in traded volumes across the NGLs market has been robust in Europe and Asia. In the propane market, volumes have continued to rise, with average monthly volumes in European propane rising by around 60% year-on-year in 2025 compared to 2024 levels. The latest Exchange data shows that total European propane volumes were around 6,000 contracts per month in 2025. Volumes have also increased in the Far East Index propane, with overall volumes rising 11% compared to 2024 levels in 2025, with total volumes of around 30,000 contracts per month. Prices tied back to Mont Belvieu have become a popular option for companies as they look to trade the spreads between the hubs in Europe or Asia and the U.S. Gulf, traders say.

As the U.S. has become a key LPG exporter due to increased shale gas production, the influence of Mont Belvieu LPG pricing also grew and was further connected with international markets. Prices across major markets like the U.S., Asia, and Europe exhibit strong long-term correlations. However, the inter-regional price spreads between the U.S., Asia, and Europe have become volatile, in part due to the changing freight rates and the various supply and demand factors that affect pricing in one region compared to another.

Volatility in these spreads has a direct impact on arbitrage opportunities for exporting cargoes from one region to another. Typically, a discounted price in the U.S. compared to Asia will see higher volumes of exports from the U.S. terminals, analysts have suggested.

Europe Purchases More U.S. Cargoes

As Europe has diversified away from long-term suppliers such as Russia, it has increased imports from regions like the U.S., where production has risen sharply. Total exports of U.S. NGLs to Europe have risen to around 135 million barrels in 2025, an increase of more than 15% year-on-year compared to 2024, according to the latest data from ship tracking firm Vortexa. The higher flows of U.S. NGLs can be hedged using the Mont Belvieu financial benchmark and any regional basis contracts, such as the Argus delivered contract in northwest Europe.

Asia’s Petrochemicals Usage Boosts Demand for U.S. NGLs

Growing propane consumption in Asia, which accounts for about 60% of the world’s population, has been the growth engine for global LPG demand. Both Japan and South Korea have been key import markets where propane is consumed as a chemical raw material and for industrial and commercial uses. Japan’s LPG demand, however, has been in a long-term decline due to factors such as a shrinking population and decarbonization efforts. Nonetheless, the country is expected to remain a key buyer for U.S. propane as it has pivoted away from Middle East suppliers, whose share of the Japanese market is currently down to around 10%.

In recent years, LPG demand has been driven by production capacity increases in Chinese Propane Dehydrogenation Units (PDH), which use propane to make a number of other downstream petrochemical products. According to the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies, PDH capacity in China reached around 22 million tons per year in 2024, accounting for roughly 32% of China’s propylene capacity, compared to 5% 10 years ago. This rapid growth in propane demand has been largely met by the rise in U.S. exports. However, going forward, the country’s PDH sector is expected to see a significant slowdown in expansion given excess capacity and weak economic fundamentals.

On the other hand, India has emerged as another key import market, where LPG plays a vital role as a primary cooking fuel. In a related development focused on fuel diversification, Indian state refiners extended the deadline for a long-term tender to import LPG from the United States in 2026. This move is part of a plan to source more cooking gas from the U.S., which would help reduce India’s heavy reliance on Middle Eastern producers, who currently supply over 90% of its import needs.

While the shifting dynamics of Asia's three largest markets present challenges, the sheer combined demand will continue to make the region a vital destination for U.S. LPG exports. The latest data from Vortexa shows NGL imports into Asia by product. Based on this data, total exports to Asia have been robust in the past 12 months. Total exports from the U.S. reached 520 million barrels in 2025, a slight increase compared to 2024 levels. Propane accounts for about 60% of total export volumes, in part due to the growth of the PDH plants, which process propane and produce other chemicals like ethylene.

U.S. Mont Belvieu to Remain a Strong Global Benchmark

Mont Belvieu has established its position as the global pricing benchmark for NGLs. A domestic surge in supply coupled with increased exports from the U.S. has integrated Mont Belvieu pricing into international trade, with its references increasingly being used in major consumption hubs in Asia and Europe. As market participants across the globe look to manage their NGL exposure, the importance of derivatives tied to this benchmark will likely continue to grow.

data.title: Silex Systems: Next-Gen Technology In The Uranium Industry
data.description:
Silex Systems Limited (OTCQX: SILXY) has strong fundamentals to advance its laser-based uranium enrichment technology amid the urgent need for nuclear generation capacity. Though the company failed to benefit from the DoE's $2.7 billion fund allocation, its subsidiary remains committed to the commercialization of the SILEX technology. The planned Paducah Laser Enrichment Facility provides up to $1 billion in revenue visibility beyond 2030, which puts SILXY stock at an attractive valuation entry point for the next-generation uranium enrichment technology. I am bullish on Silex Systems mainly because of two key points. Global Laser Enrichment unit has already achieved the TRL-6 standard, validating that SILEX technology could successfully operate. At the same time, the SILEX approach could take a unique position in the U.S. nuclear fuel supply chain by recovering the depleted uranium tails back to the natural level.

Company Background

The rapid development of artificial intelligence technology made it clear that the world requires a significant amount of baseload power generation sources. The data center build-out is outpacing the generation capacity additions by far, which brings national energy security to the forefront. In such an environment, the nuclear industry is entering a new stage of renaissance with advanced reactor and fuel technologies.

The USA operates the largest installed base of nuclear power reactors, with a capacity of near to 100 GW. In the same time, the inbound enrichment facilities of Urenco USA are capable of 4.3 million SWU, or one-third of the U.S. nuclear power reactors' needs, while Centrus Energy (LEU) commands a license for its Piketon facility for 11,500 centrifuges with a capacity of 3.8 million SWU per year.

In the meantime, laser-based uranium enrichment is a next-gen technology, representing a revolutionary process compared to the conventional centrifuge systems. Silex Systems is advancing the technology through its 51% owned subsidiary Global Laser Enrichment (“GLE”) and the planned Paducah Laser Enrichment Facility (“PLEF”). The new approach could enhance the domestic nuclear fuel supply chain by offering efficiency, reduced facility footprints, and lower operational costs.

The U.S. is a major importer of enriched uranium, which creates a strong government push to reshore reactor fuel production and eliminate dependence, especially on the Russian state-owned firm Rosatom. As a result, the Department of Energy recently awarded $2.7 billion in task orders to rebuild domestic enrichment capacity for both low-enriched uranium and high-assay low-enriched uranium.

But what could have been a primary bullish factor for Silex Systems turned out to be very disappointing for the company's investors, which pushed the SILXY stock price down by 20% since the beginning of the year. Namely, the funding was distributed evenly between Centrus Energy, General Matters, and Orano. The GLE subsidiary received merely an award of up to $28 million to advance next-generation laser-based uranium enrichment technology.

The main reason could be bad timing. The centrifuge approach is the proven and current technology, while the laser-based isotope separation lacks commercial application. Coupled with the strategic imperative to decouple from the Russian fuel and the urgent need for electricity to power the AI data center infrastructure, the Silex Systems remained sidelined.

Market Potential

Though GLE was not selected by the DOE for a $900 million award under the Low Enriched Uranium request for proposals, the company remains strongly committed to its mission to advance its unique laser-based isotope excitation process. Moreover, Silex Systems achieved a key milestone in October last year by becoming the first company to enrich uranium using laser technology, which meets the DoE Technology Readiness Level 6 (TRL-6) standard. As a result, the company made a further step towards full-scale deployment of the next-gen enrichment technology, with the main focus now shifting to preliminary commercial plant activities.

The TRL-7 would be a crucial step for GLE, potentially leading to the final investment decision required prior to PLEF construction. For this reason, the subsidiary bought a 700-acre land parcel situated adjacent to the former Paducah Gaseous Diffusion Plant. Given the contract terms between GLE and DoE for the acquisition of over 200,000 metric tonnes of legacy depleted uranium inventories in the form of DUF6, the company has exclusive access to the feedstock for the production of natural-grade uranium hexafluoride (UF6) with the SILEX technology. The Paducah depleted tails are relatively high-assay, containing uranium-235 isotopes ranging from 0.30% to 0.70%.

Hence, the GLE facility plans to generate around 2,300 metric tons of natural-grade uranium annually for up to 30 years, effectively acting as a Tier 1 uranium mine. For reference, the U.S. reactors require around 19,000 metric tons of uranium annually, which means that PLEF could support roughly 10% of total domestic reactor demand.

This makes the PLEF an extremely valuable asset for the American nuclear fuel supply chain, since it boosts the domestic enrichment capacity, reduces dependence on international suppliers, and alleviates the DOE's depleted uranium stockpiles management. In addition, the process includes the value of conversion, or 2,000 metric tons capacity annually, which is a critical supply constraint component for the U.S. nuclear industry as well. As a result, the PLEF UF6 opportunity provides for up to $500 million in revenue visibility annually for up to 30 years.

Going forward, the NRC accepted the GLE's application for the PLEF full license, establishing an 18-month review schedule with the final decision expected in the beginning of 2027. If approved, the license would authorize GLE to construct and operate the Paducah facility to re-enrich depleted uranium tails up to 8% uranium-235 content, which positions the company in the LEU and LEU+ assay levels.

The planned facility is expected to have an initial LEU capacity of 2 million SWU per year to process DoE materials. The long-term SWU price has solid fundamentals for growth, and at current levels of around $180/SWU, this stipulates an additional circa $350 million in revenue per year from the PLEF LEU/LEU+ segment. The facility is designed to scale up to 6 million SWU if market demand grows. In addition, the SILEX technology could be used for HALEU production at a U235 assay of up to 20% to supply enriched uranium fuel for the next-generation advanced nuclear reactors and SMRs.

Looking at the market potential, the rough calculations suggest that the U.S. nuclear fleet consumes 2,900 tonnes of enriched uranium at 3.5% U-235 assay, which calls for 13 million SWU. The current administration envisages expanding U.S. reactor capacity up to 400 GW by 2050. Such an expansion would require up to 11.6 tonnes of enriched uranium at 3-5% U-235 assay and a 50 million SWU capacity. At a price of $160 to $180 per SWU, the long-term LEU opportunity from the national reactors stands at around $9 billion.

Concerning the competition, apart from Urenco USA, Centrus Energy commands a license for its Piketon facility for 11,500 centrifuges with a capacity of 3.8 million SWU per year, which could be upgraded to 7 million SWU. As a result, I see a strong market demand potential for Silex Systems, which has up to $1 billion in revenue visibility at the current stage once the PLEF operation commences in 2030.

In my view, the higher enrichment efficiency of the SILEX technology places GLE in a favorable position relative to competitors using the legacy centrifuge technology. Though laser excitation technology offers a modest energy consumption improvement compared to centrifuge facilities, the next-gen technology requires a smaller physical footprint and capital expense. The SILEX technology offers a potential operating savings as well, since the process handles more material in a much smaller number of phases.

And given that PLEF is estimated to cost more than $1 billion, it's now become clear how important the DoE's $900 million award would have been. But, with the growing interest in nuclear power from data center operators and technology giants, the nuclear supply chain could attract more investment injections apart from the federal funds.

Investment Conclusion

Silex Systems booked $13.7 million in revenue in FY2025, where most of the revenue came from existing services and R&D activities. Though the company doesn't generate any revenues from its SILEX laser enrichment technology, the PLEF UF6 and LEU/LEU+ revenue visibility of $850 million puts Silex stock price at a sales valuation ratio of 1.5x. For reference, Centrus trades at a 12.5x TTM P/S ratio, while BWX Technologies (BWXT) is valued at a 6.1x sales ratio.

Looking forward, GLE has a royalty model, providing Silex Systems with a long-term revenue stream independent of its equity stake in the venture and an additional agreement of $20 million dependent on the commercial development milestones of the project. The royalty could range from 7% to 12% of gross revenue generated by GLE from the utilization of SILEX technology.

I believe that the SILEX uranium enrichment technology has the potential to become a significant contributor to the U.S. fuel supply chain. The molecular laser isotope separation approach is perfectly adapted to the production of uranium, including natural grade UF6, LEU and LEU+, and HALEU.

The project carries a high risk of the development and the commercialization of the next-generation uranium enrichment technology, which may turn private sector investors hesitant to undertake. But we have to mention two points that greatly de-risk the FLEF project. First of all, the GLE already has a test campus running in Wilmington, proving the technological feasibility and reliability of the SILEX approach. And the second is that GLE could take a unique position in the U.S. nuclear fuel supply chain by recovering the valuable U235 content from what would eventually be treated as waste back to the natural level of 0.7%. Hence, the PLEF project could facilitate the DOE's waste disposal burden and expand the domestic uranium hexafluoride capacity.

Apart from the reactor fuel, the technology is useful for the healthcare and semiconductor industries, as the specific medical isotopes and silicon could also be handled with the SILEX approach. As a result, the SILEX technology could be one of the most prominent nuclear investment ideas in the long term. Though 2030 is far away, I am bullish on SILXY stock, as the company's revenue visibility provides an attractive valuation entry point.

data.title: McCormick: A Dividend Aristocrat Priced For Perfection
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

McCormick (MKC) is a Dividend Aristocrat that’s down significantly from their peak from a few years ago, with the stock being pressured by macro factors such as tariffs and consumer weakness, but I believe the valuation is still far from an attractive entry level, currently trading above its fair value.

Over the first 9 months of 2025, MKC reported an overall solid $420.2 million in cash flow from operations, with a significant drop in CAPEX as they quickly adapted to the environment. This is an improvement over 2024 despite the lower cash flow from operations, getting to a free cash flow of $282.1 million over 9M’25 compared to $273.9 million in 9M’24.

As they mention, the cash flow from operations was weaker because of the higher working capital impact, but they expect 2025 to result in strong cash flow thanks to Q4.

The company’s free cash flow has been quite volatile for similar reasons in the past, with 2022-2024 free cash flows of $389.5 million, $973.4 million, and $647 million, respectively, going through the supply chain crisis lows and then jumping to a record high as inventories unwound in 2022. Back in 2023, for example, roughly 52% (~$500.6 million) of their free cash flow came in Q4 based on their older reports.

Financially, based on MKC’s latest report, we see some issues in terms of liquidity, with very little cash and current assets generally below their current liabilities, but the long-term debt is manageable even though $757.3 million of it was current in Q3.

Something important to note is that the equity is very far from the $18.18 billion - especially if we take out the goodwill - but the company ultimately relies on an asset-light business model, with brands and partnerships being the core behind their operations, making the equity and PB less relevant in this case.

This means that the company basically has “hidden” assets (e.g. their brands) that can be sold if they really need the cash and can’t get it in other ways.

As for the guidance, MKC maintained their net sales growth but lowered their expectations of adjusted operating income growth and EPS to 2% to 4% and $3.00 to $3.05 per share (reported, not constant currency), respectively, with cost inflation in the low- to mid-single-digits even when excluding tariffs.

The company's next earnings report is scheduled for January 22, 2026, with EPS and revenue expectations of $0.88 and $1.85 billion, respectively. I’m looking forward to more comments about the environment and their plans to adapt, as I believe macro factors have played and will continue to play a very important role in the generally weak performance seen in recent years.

Dividends & Buybacks

MKC currently pays a ~2.8% dividend that should cost about $517 million per year, recently increasing it to $0.48 per quarter, getting McCormick to 40 consecutive years of dividend growth and placing them a decade away from being a Dividend King.

This is among the highest yield we’ve seen the company trade at in recent history, and they also do some small buybacks on top, but we should keep in mind that this is getting quite tough given the pressure on the free cash flow. Based on 2024’s free cash flow, we’re looking at a roughly 80% payout ratio, which is not terrible if things improve.

Risks & Opportunities

As MKC highlights, the environment has some key areas of pressure, including the weaker consumer seen in the US and China, with tariffs impacting their affordability. As one BNP Paribas analyst mentioned, MKC is seen as a major beneficiary of Trump's tariff reversal from November 2025, with the company primarily sourcing its pepper and other spices from outside the US.

Before these improvements, MKC had been actively working on sourcing and productivity initiatives with cost savings and surgical pricing on top. I’ll also highlight that McCormick’s management mentioned very important insights into their strategy regarding tariffs during their Q3 Earnings Call from October:

Our global manufacturing location strategy, resilient supply chain, global sourcing capabilities and collaborative efforts across the organization continue to be competitive advantages, enabling us to mitigate the impact of tariff and tariff-related costs and maintain business momentum. We are absorbing some incremental costs this year, which has a near-term impact on our profitability. This approach enables us to maintain our volume momentum and sustain investment in our growth initiatives while still delivering operating profit growth for the year.

However, the subsequent consumer weakness caused by all the economic pressure seen in recent years, especially in the US can still have significant direct and indirect impacts for MKC and its clients/partners, as they also depend on foodservice demand and have much more than just the “essential” spices. The company ultimately relies on consumption, and it isn’t a surprise that they aren’t performing that well during times when discretionary budgets are lower.

This goes well beyond just tariffs, and we saw studies such as a recent one from Allianz highlighting how nearly half of Americans faced more stress at the end of 2025 compared to its beginning, with main factors including lower income, job insecurity, and rising costs of living, with fear and uncertainty persisting into 2026.

Another survey found that the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index improved a bit in December, but we should keep in mind that it comes after November 2025 was the second-lowest level seen in the history of the survey, with pressure continuing to affect the environment and ultimately being a key factor behind the weak reports seen from most companies that rely on consumers.

I believe it’s important to reiterate that this weakness is not a company-specific thing, as even the giants in most consumer-related industries reported issues in the US and China in recent years.

Another risk to keep in mind will come from the competition, and I don’t mean only the classic producers. Sauces and spices can face significant pressure from the growing trends of private label brands, with titans like Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Kroger (KR), for example, developing very powerful brands that can come with more attractive prices.

This means not only pricing pressure since these companies are able to replicate what MKC sells to some degree at lower prices by cutting the middleman and lowering marketing costs significantly, but also less shelf space and more competition.

One key way McCormick can still achieve growth is through margin improvements thanks to moves like the very recent acquisition of the controlling stake (getting from 50% to 75%) in their Mexican joint venture that produces various sauces, condiments, spices, and teas, being a leading player in Mexico. This way, they gain more exposure to a higher-margin and higher-growth business that delivers, with MKC expecting it to be accretive to adjusted operating margins in 2026.

Although this also means even more exposure to tariffs, they don’t have to be sold to the US. As they also highlighted, this can be only the “beginning” (I put this in quotes because the business dates back to 1947) of a potential hub outside of the US into Mexico and Latin America, helping them limit the negative impacts of a potential retaliation from the region.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Unilever (UL), B&G Foods (BGS), Kraft Heinz (KHC), General Mills (GIS), and Kerry Group (KRYAY), we see a clearly higher valuation from almost every point of view, with MKC generally being more expensive than peers, which has been the case for quite some time because of the overall stability and quality of the business, with the fact that they are a Dividend Aristocrat certainly helping.

On the bright side, the company can use this relatively elevated valuation to acquire peers and potentially deliver per share accretion (growth above the dilution this would generate), but that’s ultimately up to the management.

Risks Reiterated

Despite being relatively defensive and the business quality being arguably good, MKC will still depend on the environment and the consumer, while their valuation is above their peers, which assumes quite good performance despite the current environment.

Despite the November tariff reversal helping them, we should remember the company's heavy reliance on international markets for core ingredients, exposing them to any potential re-escalation/renewal of tariffs or currency headwinds.

Valuation

For McCormick's valuation, I'm starting with $625 million in free cash flow in 2025 to take into account the impact of tariffs being slightly offset by their growth compared to 2024. Let’s assume a 5% CAGR over the next 5 years thanks to potential improvements in the macro environment (including lower tariff impacts, lower debt costs, demand recovery), followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 7.5% discount rate and 269.3 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $14.82 billion, for an intrinsic value of $55.03 per share, which is below the current ~$67.72. Even if we use a starting free cash flow that’s above their recent averages, we’re still below the current level by over 10%, so the market has quite high expectations, in my opinion.

Conclusions

I rate MKC a Hold, as the stock is still trading at quite a heavy premium, with a low yield despite the high payout ratio and significant exposure to the economy. In this environment and at current prices, given how many better options we can find in the broader market from any other point of view, I don’t see a reason to go for a stock like MKC, although they’re a solid contender to add on bigger dips.

data.title: Vision 2030: How Mining Could Power Saudi Arabia's New Economy
data.description:
As Saudi Arabia accelerates its economic diversification under Vision 2030, the mining sector is emerging as a cornerstone of the Kingdom's new growth model. Ambitious reforms, increased exploration budgets and a strategic focus on gold and copper are positioning Saudi Arabia to become a global mining leader. However, the sector's rapid expansion faces challenges from resource constraints, workforce development and heightened environmental, social and governance expectations.

The take

– Saudi Arabia's minesite exploration budget increased to $146 million in 2025 from $21 million in 2022.

– In 2025, 72% of the total exploration budget went to gold and 23% to copper, reflecting the Kingdom's evolving priorities and global market demand.

– The Kingdom hosts 24 exploration and 17 advanced projects, with copper and gold reserves expected to last 15 and 40 years, respectively, amid challenges in maintaining and expanding these reserves.

– The number of projects drilled in Saudi Arabia increased to 160 in 2024 from 58 in 2023, driven by Vision 2030 initiatives, regulatory reforms, heightened investor interest and improved infrastructure.

– Saudi Arabia is in the final implementation stage of launching a metals exchange to support the mining sector with financial tools, price stability and integration with global commodity markets.

Sector transformation and strategic focus

Saudi Arabia is experiencing a transformative shift in its mining sector, propelled by its Mining Strategy — a key pillar of the Kingdom's Vision 2030 aimed at diversifying its economy and reducing reliance on oil. Substantial increases in exploration budgets, particularly for gold and copper, reflect the Kingdom's strategic priorities and ambition to unlock new mineral resources. Supported by advanced technologies and a robust pipeline of projects, Saudi Arabia is positioning itself as an emerging leader in global mining, fostering sustainable growth and attracting significant foreign investment. The Kingdom's mineral wealth is now estimated at over $2.4 trillion, up 90% since 2016, following comprehensive surveys that have revealed new discoveries beyond traditional mineral belts, including rare earth elements and battery metals.

Exploration budget trends

Saudi Arabia's commitment to expanding its mining sector is evident in the substantial year-over-year increases in exploration budgets since 2022. The minesite exploration budget surged 595%, reaching $146 million in 2025 from $21 million in 2022. This growth is driven by ongoing geological surveys, technological advancements and higher exploration budgets, all of which signal stability and opportunity, attracting foreign investment. While the growth is striking, exploration spending remains modest compared to established mining jurisdictions such as Australia and Canada, which typically allocate over $1 billion annually.

Commodity focus and market dynamics

The allocation of exploration budgets by commodity has shifted significantly, reflecting Saudi Arabia's evolving priorities and market dynamics. Since 2022, the share allocated to gold has risen sharply, commanding approximately 72% of the total exploration budget in 2025. This underscores gold's importance as a driver of sector growth, foreign investment and economic diversification. Copper accounted for 23% of the 2025 budget, recognizing its rising global demand, especially for energy transition and infrastructure development. By prioritizing gold and copper, Saudi Arabia is positioning itself to capitalize on future market opportunities and drive long-term growth. Other commodities, such as silver, potash and lanthanides, still attract investment, but their relative share has declined, driven by promising new deposits and strategic responses to international market trends. Saudi Arabia aims to enter the top 20 for copper- and gold-producing countries globally.

Project pipeline and regulatory reforms

Minesite exploration accounted for 62% of the 2025 exploration budget, with these funds going toward expanding existing operations, increasing resource estimates and supporting near-term production growth. Focusing spending on minesite activities shows an emphasis on yielding quick results. It also allows reserves near ongoing operations to be brought into production more rapidly.

Saudi Arabia hosts a robust pipeline of 24 exploration and 17 advanced projects. Its proactive approach to geological surveys and resource assessment has uncovered significant potential across gold, copper, phosphate and bauxite. Substantial government investment and advanced technologies support this project pipeline. Additionally, a new mining investment law has reduced the tax rate to 20% from 45%, enhancing investor confidence and aligning regulations with global standards, which accelerates mineral development in line with Vision 2030. In its ninth licensing round, Saudi Arabia awarded 172 mining sites across three mineral-rich belts, attracting about 671 million Saudi Arabian riyals (US$179 million) in pledged investments and marking the largest licensing round in its history. The 10th round, covering 13,000 sq km, will be announced at the Future Minerals Forum in Riyadh on Jan. 13–15, 2026.

Mineral reserves and project activity

The Kingdom's mining sector is underpinned by substantial mineral reserves, with copper estimated at 15 years of reserve life and gold at 40 years, providing long-term security for ongoing operations and enhancing investor attractiveness. A lengthy gold reserve life enhances Saudi Arabia's role as a key player in the global gold industry.

In 2024, Saudi Arabia experienced a marked increase in drilling activity, with the number of projects rising to 160 from 58 in 2023. Vision 2030 targets increasing mining's GDP contribution to 240 billion Saudi Arabian riyals (US$64 billion) by 2030, creating 200,000 direct and indirect jobs, and attracting US$27 billion in new investment. Key policies include the new mining investment law, streamlined permitting, enhanced geological surveys and incentives for foreign investors. These measures are designed to foster a competitive, transparent and sustainable mining industry. The country is also leveraging its mineral wealth to develop downstream processing capacity and secure feedstock for green industrial projects, both domestically and through strategic investments globally, such as in Africa.

Ma'aden and international partnerships

State-owned Saudi Arabian Mining Co. (Ma'aden) is central to Saudi Arabia's mining ambitions, leading major projects and joint ventures. Key operations include the Jabal Sayid copper mine in Madina with Barrick Mining Corp.; the Al Ba'itha bauxite mine in Al Qassim province and the Ras Al Khair aluminum complex in Ash Sharqīyah (the Eastern province) with Alcoa Corp.; and large-scale phosphate mining at the Wa'ad Al Shamal mine in Al Ḩudūd ash Shamālīyah with The Mosaic Co. While Ma'aden and its established partners remain dominant, interest from Asia, Europe and North America is growing, with companies exploring partnerships, technology transfer and investment opportunities in gold, copper and industrial minerals. These international players are drawn by Saudi Arabia's resource potential, supportive regulatory reforms and the strategic importance of the Arabian Shield. A mineral-rich geological region in western Saudi Arabia, the Arabian Shield is known for its ancient rocks and significant deposits of gold, copper and other metals. Ma'aden has also established a joint venture with Hancock Prospecting Pty Ltd. to accelerate exploration in the Nabita Ad-Duwayhi Gold-Belt, covering over 24,000 sq km, further strengthening international collaboration and exploration efforts.

Challenges and outlook

Despite significant regulatory reforms that have elevated Saudi Arabia's position in the global mining industry, ongoing improvements in transparency and governance are essential to meet international standards. Sustained success will depend on overcoming several ESG challenges. The Arabian Shield, central to Vision 2030's diversification strategy, remains underexplored. High exploration spending does not always guarantee discoveries due to geological complexity, limited historical data and technical challenges. Operational risks are heightened by water scarcity and infrastructure gaps in remote mining regions, requiring substantial investment in desalination, recycling and the development of roads, power and communications. Additionally, workforce shortages and logistical issues could slow project timelines. Price volatility and the need for robust financial tools are being addressed through the new metals exchange, which aims to provide stability and attract global expertise. Ultimately, maintaining momentum and attracting investment will hinge on responsible resource management and effective ESG practices, as global investors increasingly prioritize environmental stewardship, workforce safety and community engagement.

As Saudi Arabia continues to implement reforms and attract global investment, the mining sector is poised for substantial growth. Overcoming ESG challenges will be critical to realizing Vision 2030's ambitions and establishing the Kingdom as a global mining leader. With the launch of the metals exchange, expanded licensing and high-profile events like the Future Minerals Forum, Saudi Arabia is advancing its vision to become a hub for mineral trading, processing and responsible supply chains — both regionally and globally.

Original Post

data.title: Royal Bank Of Canada: It's Still A Dividend Play But Expensive
data.description:
It has been three months since I previously covered Royal Bank of Canada (RY). Its value has already risen by 15% despite my cautious stance. As a new investor, I am thankful that I decided to follow my plan to buy some of its stocks and test it for a while. I understand the optimistic market sentiments considering its robust performance recently. However, I still believe that this is not yet the right time to disregard downside risks as upside potential becomes narrower. Valuation is already high today despite some flaws in my previous article. Technicals are still bullish, but recent overbuying and insider selling may warrant some waiting time to find a better entry point.

RY Q4 2025: A Solid Ending Amid Macroeconomic Volatility

The macroeconomic conditions in the US and Canada have improved in the past three months. However, volatility is still present due to stubborn inflation and tariff concerns. As a bank, Royal Bank of Canada is still susceptible to headwinds due to its sensitivity to economic changes and cyclical nature. Even so, it continues to demonstrate its fundamental strength and prove why it’s one of the Canadian Big Five Banks. In its most recent performance, we saw how it navigated a volatile market environment with ease.

In Q4 2025, its interest income, the primary revenue component of RY, remained stable and flat on a YoY and QoQ basis. It amounted to CAD 26.3B, down by 0.8% YoY from CAD 26.5B. Yet, it increased by 0.7% from my previous coverage at CAD 26.1B. If we look at it on a YoY basis, it is easy to point out that the policy easing cycle impacted the yields of its interest-earning assets. It is most visible in loan interest, which decreased by 1.5%. After all, lower interest rates mean lower interest income on variable-rate loans.

On a lighter note, the interest rate cut raised loan demand. Both retail and whole loans rose. Overall, the loan base increased YoY and QoQ. This allowed the company to stay resilient as it regained its strength from Q3 to Q4 2025. Also, it benefited from higher yields on securities. Moreover, lower rates enticed more banking customers, so RY enjoyed higher commissions and fees. So, its non-interest income increased and completely offset interest income. Lastly, lower interest rates lowered the interest on deposits and borrowings. As a result, the total revenue reached CAD 17.2B, up by 14.2% YoY from CAD 15.1B and by 1.4% from CAD 17.0B in my previous coverage.

Risk Factors

RY is a solid bank if you ask me. But we should still know that it’s not completely shielded from various headwinds. Inflation may still impact the repayment capacity of borrowers. Tariff woes may exacerbate this since the extra costs incurred by domestic producers may be passed on to consumers. This may lead to higher prices in the market and affect the finances of businesses and consumers. So, delinquencies and defaults are also possible.

Mitigants

Despite the potential risks, I believe that RY is still well-positioned. As we can see, it benefits from the recent policy easing cycle. The move by the Fed may somehow offset the impact of inflation. Why? Lower interest rates increase borrower and investor confidence. This is why its loan base increased last quarter. Also, lower rates boost economic activity, which may raise employment and wages. As a result, consumers and borrowers may still keep up with the rising prices. This may also allow them to repay borrowings on time.

Moreover, RY maintains its strategic fundamental positioning to cushion the impact of macroeconomic headwinds and protect its business in case of a crisis.

One essential consideration here is its well-diversified assets. Like the other Big Five banks I’ve recently covered, RY does not solely rely on loans for its capital base and revenues. In fact, it maintains the balance between growth and stability. As you can see, only 53% of its interest-earning assets come from loans. To be honest, I think this is much lower than a typical percentage of 70%, 80%, or 90% since it’s a bank. This shows that it has multiple revenue streams.

Meanwhile, investment securities comprise 29%. The remaining 18% comes from its deposits in other banks and securities purchased under reverse repurchase agreements. I like this diversification strategy because it allows RY to be flexible with growth and risks. It also spreads risks because the weakness in one interest-earning asset can be offset by another. If you look at its income statement, interest income on investment securities rose by CAD 883M. This completely offset the decrease in interest on loans and deposits.

Its loan base is also well diversified. Its wholesale loans cover various sectors, allowing it to be exposed to multiple opportunities. Note that some sectors are not very sensitive to inflation and tariffs. These may include oil and gas, government, public works and infrastructure, and telecommunication. We should also note that wholesale loans are mainly for large projects by governments and businesses. More or less, there is little to no exposure to consumer loans. Meanwhile, its consumer loan exposure in the retail segment is minimal. In retail loans, personal loans and credit cards only comprise 22% of the total. If we add the wholesale loans, the actual consumer loan exposure is only 14%.

Now, why do I think it matters? First, businesses and government are more resilient and resistant to inflation. They are highly capable of repaying borrowings on time. Second, business loans are typically secured and collateralized, unlike credit card loans. Considering this, it should not be surprising that its credit quality in Canada remains stable and decent. Total delinquencies are still low at 0.40% and the same as in Q4 2024 and Q3 2025.

Lastly, its loan split between fixed and variable rates is strategic. For instance, only 35% of its Canadian Residential Mortgage Portfolio has variable rates. The remaining 65% of these loans have fixed rates. This allows RY to limit the unfavorable impact of the recent rate cut. Only that portion is subject to lower repricing rates. This can be another reason why the interest income on loans remains manageable despite the rate cut by the Fed and BoC.

And for its capital adequacy, RY remains well capitalized. Its 13.5% CET1 Ratio shows that its highest-quality capital can cover that portion of risk-weighted assets. If I add the other Tier 1 capital, the ratio will be 15.1%. And if I add the remaining capital, the Total Capital Ratio will reach 16.8%. This is higher YoY and QoQ. This is also more than twice the Basel III requirement of 8.0%. With that, it is safe to assume that RY has enough capital buffer. It can survive a recession and remain liquid without resorting to borrowing. It can also stay operational even if deposit runs happen. This is supported by its low Loan-To-Deposit Ratio of 69%. This proves that RY has excess reserves since it does not use more than 30% of deposits for loans. Also, the remaining interest-earning assets, like deposits and investment securities, are liquid.

Valuation

As I revisited its valuation, I realized some flaws in my previous coverage.

First, price ratios show potential stock overvaluation as RY trades above its five-year average. P/E shows that the stock trades at 16.87x its EPS versus the average of 13.79x. If I use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $138.29. The same applies to P/B and P/S, which suggest TPs of $132.60 and $133.24, respectively.

However, this method does not always assure us of the price direction. After all, price ratios only reveal whether the stock is cheaper or more expensive than its historical levels. The market may not care about it that much. And as long as the EPS/revenue/book value increases, the price will most likely follow. The same happened to the stock price of RY.

Nonetheless, we cannot deny the fact that the stock is already a bit too high for a buy now. Risks must still be considered. With that, I still believe that buying at this price is not reasonable.

For a more forward-looking analysis, I also used the Dividend Discount Model, or DDM. The annualized dividend is now $4.73 versus $4.39 in my previous coverage. So, the growth rate is now 6.27% based on the six-year CAGR on Seeking Alpha. I still set its risk premium at 5.0% to be realistic. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00, assuming the stock to be in line with the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, my new TP is $161.43. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $153.36.

Now, why did my DDM TP increase? First, the dividend growth rate increased from 6.0% in my previous coverage to 6.27%. Second, Beta decreased from 1.04 to 1.00 in line with the S&P 500. Third, the cost of equity decreased to 9.20% due to the decrease in Beta. The new TP gives logic to the recent price rally. However, I am expecting some downside risks since the price has already exceeded my new TP.

Technicals

RY’s technicals are still bullish as the price increases with higher highs and lows. It moves above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing that uptrend continuation is still possible. Why? The current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA also trends upwards, suggesting that market sentiments are still optimistic.

But MACD and RSI show some downside risks. The MACD line is now trending downwards below the signal line. This demonstrates decreasing buying volume and/or increasing selling volume. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume in the past week has already outpaced buying volume. RSI adheres to this as it shows overbuying. This may lead to a dip due to profit-taking. With that, I advise investors to wait on the sidelines as we await profit-takers to move and set new support.

Conclusion

Royal Bank of Canada remains a solid banking stock with robust fundamentals and strategic asset diversification. It is well-positioned against macroeconomic headwinds due to its high capital adequacy and excess reserves. However, valuation is still too high for my appetite and appears quite unreasonable today. Technicals adhere to it as the stock becomes overbought, which may lead to profit-taking and a dip. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: U.S. Export Coal Extends Rally To Close Out 2025
data.description:
US coal market prices edged higher in December, extending a steady rally dating back to October. The gains to year-end 2025 accrued mainly to export benchmarks.

Through 2027, firm natural gas prices are expected to support increased coal generation, driving stable or growing coal production. After 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity, incentivized in part by broad-based tax credits. Overall, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, with much of the planned retirement activity deferred until after 2030.

Coal plant generation share through 2035 is forecast to decline slowly to just 7.5% from 18.7% of total generation in 2026. The rapid phaseout of tax credits under the recently approved US tax and spending bill — also referred to as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" — is forecast to slow wind and solar generation growth, allowing coal plants to run more often after 2030. The most production uplift will take place at the Powder River Basin (PRB), followed by the Illinois Basin (ILB), as the reduced growth of wind and solar generation mainly affects the generation mix in the Midcontinental US.

The take

  • US coal prices again moved higher in December, even as production and shipments trended essentially flat year over year for the month.
  • The S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast anticipates 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, amid market pressure to retain existing generation due to greater reliability needs and slower forecast growth in solar and wind energy.
  • The production outlook varies by region, with the Powder River Basin and Illinois Basin expecting stable to slightly growing production through 2027, while Appalachian coal production is forecast to decline, due to reduced domestic demand and limited export growth.

Coal prices clawed higher in December, with export benchmarks showing the most gains. CAPP region export benchmarks added $1.00/short ton to $82.00/short ton (1.2%), NYMEX CAPP added $2.00/short ton (2.6%) to $80.00/short ton, and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 Btu per pound held flat at $62.00/short ton. Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur moved $0.50/short ton higher to $51.75/short ton, while the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark held at $15.00/short ton.

The cold snap driving heating demand across the eastern US in late November mostly abated by mid-December. Henry Hub spot gas prices held mostly firm, opening December at $4.59/MMBtu and falling to a low of $3.31/MMBtu over the holiday season before rallying to close the month at $4.40/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $4.28/MMBtu for the month. Storage withdrawals continued strong through mid-December, with working gas at 3,579 Bcf as of Dec. 12 — 32 Bcf above the five-year average and 61 Bcf below the same week of 2024.

Except for the Northeast US, regional gas market prices were subdued. Chicago Gate averaged $3.91/MMBtu, a $0.37/MMBtu discount to Henry Hub. TCO Pool's discount averaged $0.44/MMBtu for a monthly average of $3.84/MMBtu, while TETCO M3 went to a premium of $1.00/MMBtu for a monthly average of $5.28/MMBtu. SoCal Border averaged $3.20/MMBtu during the month, $1.08/MMBtu below Henry Hub.

The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) estimated September 2025 coal stockpiles at 105 million short tons, essentially unchanged from August. The mild summer and associated weak coal demand provided some cushioning against subsequent drawdowns during November-December.

Current forward pricing for PRB coal is flat and stable, reflecting sufficient inventories at power plants and mining capacity to increase production as needed against firmer natural gas prices. After 2027, lower natural gas prices and declining coal demand are forecast to restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal price levels are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's price levels making domestic coal generation generally less competitive against Northeast natural gas. Firmer eastern natural gas prices nevertheless boosted eastern coal demand in the first half of 2025, mitigating a decline in Asia-Pacific seaborne coal demand compared to 2024..

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $65/short ton suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal, with Atlantic Basin exports above that threshold and coal competing in Pacific Basin export markets generally below. After declining in 2024, bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and more supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and overall eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 47 MMst in 2025–30.

Outlook for US coal production demand

For the four weeks ending Dec. 27, coal shipments averaged 9.9 MMst, 1.6% above 2024 levels. This continues the fourth-quarter trend of elevated inventories keeping shipments in line with 2024, following elevated shipments during the first half of 2025. Shipments ended 2025 at an aggregate 535 MMst, implying an inventory drawdown from year-end 2024 of 5-10 MMst.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027. Gas-to-coal switching during the first quarter of 2025 reduced coal inventory surpluses from 2024-end, setting up production growth in 2025. We now forecast 2025 coal production at 530 MMst, an increase of 18 MMst (3.4%) from 2024 levels. Coal generation is forecast to further gain market share from natural gas through 2027, until relative coal and gas pricing normalizes and expanding green energy again puts pressure on coal generation. The overall coal market, comprising domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline by 141 MMst between 2025 and 2030.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

Production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) through the third quarter of 2025 indicate production of 172.2 MMst, an annualized rate of 229.6 MMst. Production is now forecast at 230 MMst in 2025, with higher inventories potentially constraining fourth-quarter production. Production is forecast to grow through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, S&P Global Energy projects that coal retirements in the Midwest and expansion of wind generation in PRB's core markets will gradually shrink the coal demand to 192 MMst, declining further to 156 MMst through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

MSHA's September quarter 2025 production reports indicate production of 50.6 MMst, or an annualized rate of 67.5 MMst. We forecast annual production will grow to 78 MMst through 2027, after which the expansion of wind generation and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode ILB coal demand. Coal production in the ILB is forecast to fall to 60 MMst by 2030, declining further to 39 MMst by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

MSHA's production reports for the year's third quarter indicate year-to-date production at 118.8 MMst, or an annualized rate of 158.4 MMst. Appalachian coal demand tends to be more sensitive to global seaborne markets; PRB and ILB tend to be driven by domestic natural gas prices. As the latter two are forecast to experience improved demand versus natural gas generation, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast 2025 production at 159 MMst, just 0.6% higher than 2024. As remaining domestic demand erodes after 2027, with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 112 MMst by 2030.

Further information

Market indicative coal forecasts by S&P Global Energy represent forward curves for spot-traded instruments, analogous to a strip of contracts. The shorter tenors — current year and prompt year, plus additional years, if available — are driven by the observed/assessed market. The longer tenors — typically forecast years three to 20 for physically assessed markers — are driven by fundamental estimates of cash costs of production, accepted returns to capital, regional productive capacity, and forecast supply and demand. For the long-tenured portion of the curve, S&P Global Energy forecasts prices for specific coal markers and defines the remaining markers via historical spreads.
